speaker: interviewer
text: --1995 interview with Fred Spiegel in Howell, New Jersey. So you were in the star camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --With your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sternlager, yeah, yeah. What was-- what happened in-- in-- in Bergen-Belsen was that-- you know where Bergen-Belsen's located, right? It's right in-- in the middle of North Germany, you know, it's--. Eventually, more and more people came, you know. The-- like I said, the sanitary conditions never were good, but even so, at the beginning, the people managed to keep somehow clean. And the women, especially, would watch our-- us kids-- kids to make sure that we washed ourselves, you know. And as I said, the food at the beginning was like one slice of bread a day plus some terrible soup. And there was something which looked like-- which was some dirty stuff which looked like coffee, which I didn't touch, of course, but I don't know what it was made of. But whoever took it said it was awful. The day-to-day life really consisted of roll call in the morning and going to work. I didn't-- I said, I didn't in Bergen-Belsen-- most of the time, I didn't. A little bit, I did. You know, there was a time when I worked in-- they were-- they were pulling soles of shoes from shoes, things like that, you know. But most of the time, they didn't make us work, the kids. They wanted us there at the roll call, which was, you know-- sometimes if the Germans were in a bad mood, you-- they would-- you would stand there forever, because nobody escaped or that I'm-- at least not from that camp-- nobody that I'm aware of. Nobody escaped. So the roll call really was just another form of-- of trying to-- to annoy you or upset you or whatever. Another form of harassment, basically. But when we arrived, of course, it was January, which was very cold. Then the-- the spring started. So it's always-- you always feel a bit better when the weather gets warm. But then in the early spring or late-- it might have been later, May, June-- you know, as we were in the middle of Germany, the bombings were there, but not in Bergen-Belsen itself. But we could see the-- you know, in-- in the sunny days, you could see the planes high up in the sky. And in Bergen-Belsen, there was a crematorium, of course. There were no gas chambers, by the way, you know, but there was a crematorium where they burned the dead people. And there was a factory nearby where they manufactured clothes for the German army. I think they-- they-- from old rags or something, whatever. And somewhere along the line, this was considered an important target. And it wasn't attacked by bombs. It was attacked one day on-- out of the blue. It was attacked by fighter planes, you know, by-- by shootings.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember this very well because this was a big event, you know, because-- why do I say it was a big event? I mean, the camp was shot up too, at the same time. The crematorium went in flames. The camp-- some people got killed, you know, by the bullets.

speaker: interviewee
text: I always remember the sight of the-- the few German guards who were in the camp all of a sudden threw themself on the ground. They were scared. I didn't know what was happening, so I didn't [INAUDIBLE] myself on the ground. You know, I'd never been shot at from the air. And then I-- and I found out, though, in-- in our barracks also, a couple of women, you know, got-- I know one woman got-- all the bullets must have gone-- must have gone through her. Then she was, you know, killed, and others were, too. The other thing I remember in those days, something very interesting. Like I told you, it was an-- supposedly it was an exchange camp at the beginning. And these-- some of these Jews with a Palestine-- brought a certificate to go to Palestine were called. And it was in June of 1944. They were called, supposedly to be exchanged, to-- to send-- be sent to Palestine. And they were, by the way. 222 of them were called and put on a train. And this I found, of course, years later-- made their way to-- to-- by train all the way across Europe through Turkey to Syria and made it to Palestine. And this was the only [? transfer. ?] And everybody thought, OK, this is the beginning. Maybe we can get out of this hell-hole and go somewhere, maybe to Switzerland, whatever. But this was the only exchange to Palestine. Then later on, there was-- by the way, these 222 Jews were exchanged for German Templers who were living in Palestine from-- from long before the war and had been interned by the British. And for some reason, 1944, the German government wanted them back. I don't know why. You know, that's unimportant anyway. So that's how they managed to-- to leave Bergen-Belsen. Then there was supposed to be an exchange through Switzerland, and some people were called for that. We weren't, but some people were called for that. A-- I don't know how many, a few, and they were put on the train. They didn't make it to Switzerland because there was some hitch developed in-- in the exchange. But they weren't sent back to Bergen-Belsen. They were sent to a different camp near the Swiss border, like a-- internment-- internment camp. It wasn't as bad as Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was it. Then the-- the commandant changed. We got Josef Kramer to be the commandant of Bergen-Belsen, and he had been in Auschwitz, OK? And he decided that Bergen-Belsen was a concentration camp, and if-- you know, this is what it's supposed to be, and this is what it's going to be.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meanwhile, towards the fall of 1944, many more people arrived. First of all, we got more people coming from Holland. We got some people from France who came into our camp. From-- Greek, a few more Greeks. Nothing which was happening-- I explained to you at the beginning that the Greeks were in charge who from Salonica who had been there before us who received all the food and were corrupt. And-- and-- and where the-- the-- the Jewish commandant was a Greek, and there were--. In the summer, in the middle of the summer, the Germans decided that they were too corrupt or whatever. I don't know how-- I mean, how they came to that conclusion. I mean, they were obviously, but they got rid of them. You know, they weren't going to allow them to run the camp anymore. And they brought in kapos, you know, kapos from outside, not from within-- non-Jewish, mostly Poles, non-Jewish. And like I said, and-- to run the camp from within. And I remember the picture of-- there was constant roll call that day when it happened. It was like a revolution, internal revolution. Nobody knew quite what was happening. But I remember the-- the Greeks hiding in our barracks because they were afraid. And they didn't go out to roll call because they were afraid of the Germans. So we hid them in the barracks. I mean, I didn't, but they were hidden by-- by some of our people in the barracks. And of course, they kept a low profile because they were afraid that the Germans would take them away from Bergen-Belsen and send them somewhere else.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then-- this was at the time already of Kramer, OK? And then, aside from the new people coming into our camp, people were coming from east-- from the east on those famous death-- or infamous death marches-- or by trains from the camps which were being-- you know, they were close to the front, including Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by October, November of 1944, not only were conditions bad, they were worse, became worse, because they took away some of our barracks and made us double and triple up, you know, [? instead ?] of-- the bunks at the beginning were three-- three high, you know, with straw mattresses. They became four or five high. I don't know how they held up and didn't fall over, but they did. I guess they were next to each other so they wouldn't fall over. And sometimes for smaller, were two or three to a-- to a bunk. The food got to be worse, if that's possible, but it did. We got maybe one piece of bread a week. We did get this rotten soup-- it was frozen turnip soup== every day. Also, these-- like I said, the sanitary conditions were very bad. They got worse because less barracks. There were no-- there no-- no washrooms. People started getting sick. And people were dying from the typhus, you know, because we-- we-- we had lice. We had lice all over, you know, slowly but surely. I think the lice came originally, probably, with the beds, and I'm sure they were in there already. And the-- we-- we had no way of getting rid of them. And you know, lice spread disease. And one of the diseases which were-- which [INAUDIBLE] made Bergen-Belsen so bad was typhus. You know, it's-- it's a-- it's a deadly disease. Many people died or were dying. We-- like I said, we didn't-- I think on my whole stay in Bergen-Belsen, I took two showers. And to take a shower was a-- was a big thing because they had to take us out of the camp to someone else to take a shower. That's why, they-- I guess they never wanted to bother in the first place because in our part of the camp, there were no showers. Anyway, so like I said, things got worse. We had this-- continued to have this roll call. In the meanwhile, next to us were people from other camps who had walked into the-- brought into the Bergen-Belsen. And if they didn't have typhus, they had typhus by the time they got there from us. They got it from us, probably. And they were undernourished. I mean, we were also, of course, undernourished tremendously, but they were even worse. So they died. You know, like, you know, you could see the bodies lying all over the-- all over the camp and piled up in front of the barracks, you know, right to the way roof. And pits, they dug pits because the crematorium didn't function. [INAUDIBLE] Like I told you before, the crematorium burned down. It was not a good thing. You know, people might say, oh, that's good. It's not good because you couldn't burn the bodies. And we did notice at one time in October, November next door to us there were some people speaking Dutch who had come-- well, we didn't know where they came from. They had come from Auschwitz, apparently, who were actually Dutch who had survived Auschwitz. They all-- they were women, actually, who had gotten to Bergen-Belsen. You know, of course, I didn't know at the time, I didn't know it. was-- you know, Anne Frank was amongst those who-- who were in that part of the camp. And for a while, they were next door to us. And I remember you-- you couldn't see anybody because it was a barbed wire with straw in between. But you could talk to them. And I thought maybe a cousin would be there or something. So I went there to-- to look, but I didn't find anybody. Other people did find some-- some relatives there. And conditions in a way became untenable. You know, it's hard to describe so many years later, you know. I don't know how we even survived those few months because if you were lucky, you had this one slice of bread a week. You had this turnip soup. And then what we did get, I think, which may have helped, we got-- got-- once we got Red Cross parcels. In October of 1944, we got parcels from the Red Cross, which had been sent I don't know from where. And they distributed to everybody a parcel, which was some-- a few dry goods. And by the way, afterwards they found not far from Bergen-Belson a huge storage house full of those parcels which were just put there and never bought to us, you know. So this continued for those-- all those months. And really, the Germans themselves were almost afraid to come into the camp because of the disease. The kapos were there, of course, and they themself made the roll call in the morning. And I remember many times it was snowing, and it was cold, and they made us stand. And some of the kapos, when you knew them, they would have turned the other way. And I would run away and hide in one of the barracks because I didn't want to stand outside in the cold. And this helped me to survive, I guess. The counting didn't mean anything anyway because people were dying by so many that I think it was just a sham, the whole thing. We-- this continued and-- and-- for-- for December, January, February, March. I-- I-- this-- it's like-- in a way like a blur. I can't tell you, really, what I did day by day. I think day by day, it really was a struggle just to live. | Know that-- that I used to try to scrounge food. One of the things, for instance, which I did at one time was outside the barbed wire in another-- you know, across the camp-- it was part of the camp. It was a kitchen which was used-- I don't know whether it was used for-- for the Germans or for some privileged prisoners, but there was food there. And at night-- and there was-- at the beginning, some Jews from our camp were working there, and they used to bring food with them for their families. But in the end, they were thrown out and had Russian prisoners of war working there. And they were sort of surprised to see kids, you know, right across the road from them in a-- they'd never seen that before, kids in a concentration camp, you know. There was a Russian prisoner-of-war camp not far away in Bergen-Belsen. So one or-- once or twice, I-- I managed to crawl out through the barbed wire at night to the kitchen, and they gave me some-- some stale bread, you know, which was very dangerous, I think, for them and-- [INAUDIBLE] for me, of course, but for them also. You know, they weren't allowed to-- not supposed to do that. And this is really what we did. We tried to sort of find ways and means to-- to-- to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then, like I said, it was a blur. I don't really-- can't tell you anything particular of-- of that time period, just a lot of people dying, and dying, you know, just being left there or dying in the barracks or dying in their beds. I-- what I remember is that at one time, March, April, we started hearing guns in the distance, OK, which according to the German guards were British, OK? Those few who came to the camp told us these were British.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it wasn't over for us because in the beginning of April, they decided to evacuate our camp for whatever reason and put it on a train. So we were called, and we were put on the train in the first week in April, I think it was. And we had to walk. This time there were no trucks to take us. We had to walk all the way to Celle, which was very difficult because all those were sick. And we had this-- aside from some-- some of us having typhus, we had this-- you get this disease from hunger. It was called edema or something like that, but-- the-- the way your-- everything swells, you know-- your-- your your legs swell, and-- and your-- you know, your joints swell up, and-- and this is-- this comes from-- from malnutrition, from hunger. And we walked to the-- to the train. And this is one-- one of the few-- I saw-- we were guarded by German soldiers. I think they were soldiers. I don't think they were SS. I'm not sure. But one or two of the soldiers tried to help some of the smaller children-- because there were children and-- and-- and-- to-- to make it. It was one of those cattle wagons where we got on. And we were I don't know how many in a cattle wagon. It was-- the door was closed. And we traveled like that for about five days. We stopped once or twice in the middle. But once we were let out and once we went and once we-- we were at a town called Stendal, which was-- in-- and in the railroad station and was being bombed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they moved us out just as we were being bombed. And that-- after five days-- five or six days-- the train finally stopped near the Elbe. It was near the Elbe, which I didn't know it at the time, but I know it was near Magdeburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a little town, or a little village, called Hillersleben nearby. Next to the wagon, the-- the-- the-- the cattle wagon where I was were the German guards behind us. They had, like, a regular-- regular railroad car where they lived, and they had lots of food, at least lots of bread. I was looking for bread. Bread was the main thing which interested me by that time. And when we stopped, we were allowed-- we were allowed to get off the train. And all those years, I never spoke German. I didn't want to. Even if I understood and I-- I spoke, I didn't want to speak German. I spoke Dutch. Then I asked one of the German guards what-- what was happening. And he said, well, the engine went away to see-- there apparently is a-- a German-- not a German, an-- an American unit in the area. And they went to see what's happening, you know, and if the engine doesn't come back-- back by nightfall, that means the-- the Americans are coming, and they're going to leave. And then in afternoon, they had some-- they gave us-- those who were close by, they gave us some of the bread they had. They gave it to us, and they left. And then I started to walk to the village, and I knocked on the door. There-- there was nobody outside. I knocked on the doors and asked for food. And I guess I must have looked like a scarecrow, you know, because I was wearing clothes which-- which probably was-- even if I hadn't grown much, it was too small for me and was rotting and full of lice. And-- and-- and you get these-- you know, these scabs on [INAUDIBLE] over. They didn't-- they didn't even want to open the door, and when they opened the door, they said, well, we are refugees ourself. We don't have any food. And then I sat down on-- on sort of on the front stoop of somebody's house. And and some-- some American soldiers came, OK, down the street. And I think the second or third one stopped to ask me who I was. I didn't understand what he was saying because I didn't know English.

speaker: interviewee
text: So strangely enough, he spoke in-- in-- in sort of Yiddish, you know. He was-- he probably was Jewish. You know, because I understood German, and it wasn't-- wasn't clean German what he spoke. It was-- it was like Yiddish.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I told him, you know, what-- that I was-- there was a train nearby, and I-- and my sister was there. I'd been looking for food. So he started to dig out his rations and gave them to me. Then he stopped a jeep and, you know, put me on the jeep to take me back to where the train was. And then other soldiers came and-- and gave their food, you know, in order to bring it to-- to the people in-- in at the train. And of course, then the Germans saw through their windows what was happening. Then they came out and-- and bought bread or whatever. All of a sudden they had food. You know, they wanted to look good for-- to the American soldiers, I guess. So they took us back. Some of the soldiers took us back by jeep. And-- they sat off next to me like this. I'm sure they didn't want to touch me, you know. But I've never seen anything like it before, you see. I laugh now-- it wasn't really funny-- because we were really-- must have seemed like something from a different world, you know. And they had not been prepared. I mean, if anybody tells you the Americans came to liberate, they didn't come to liberate. They came to fight the war. They didn't come to liberate anybody. They came to liberate-- it-- it was by coincidence. They happened upon a train. They happened upon a camp, so they liberated the camp. But they were not ready for the kind of conditions because these are the kind of conditions which don't exist, you know. These are subhuman conditions, you know, where you have people dying, you know, on the-- on-- on in cattle-- people in cattle wagons dying. And so they came upon the train and didn't know quite what to do. You know, I mean, everybody knew we were liberated. Everybody cheered. But that was it, you know. Then we had to sort of find our own way to find food, you know, from the local German population. The Americans who were in the area did give their rations, whatever they had with them, they gave us, you know. But for that day, nothing much else happened till the next day or the day or-- the day after, when--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was approximately when?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in-- I would say around April 15, you  know, give or take. We-- [INAUDIBLE] a week after we left. Like I said, one of the German soldiers afterwards said that Roosevelt had died. So, you know, if you figure we probably didn't know this right away, and-- and-- so I think it was around April 15, OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was a week before your 13th birthday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right, right. I was-- my birthday's on April 21, yeah. Didn't-- I didn't really bother me very much, my birthday, at the time, you know. Didn't think about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, the next day or the day after, I think it was the-- it must have been the medical officer of the unit, you know, came, and he looked. And I guess he was in shock, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he decided right away that he has to evacuate the train, get everybody off that train first of all. If you want to take care of them, can't leave them on the train, you know, packed like sardines and-- and-- and-- no proper sanitation, and-- and-- and no nothing, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they started to take us to this little town where there was some buildings where-- with apartments which had been abandoned by German officers who had fled because of the advance of the American army. They put it in there together with another family, OK, another Jewish family. Or-- there was a lady with-- with her son, and I think her husband already was dead. He was, yeah. I don't even remember the name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she of course was older, so she tried to sort of, you know, look after us, you know. Wasn't [INAUDIBLE] she was very sick herself. And at one time or another, you know, when we went out, one of the soldiers would approach me and said, where are you from, and-- in German. And I said, from Holland. OK, I'll take you back tomorrow to Holland, you know. They wanted to, you know-- to be nice to us, you know, all of a sudden. I mean, they-- they didn't-- you know, they wanted to maybe-- that's the thing-- that's the solution for these poor kids, you know, running around, nothing to eat and-- and looked terrible. They were very sick. So of course, he couldn't do that. You know, I mean, this officer wouldn't allow that, and, I mean, can't just arbitrarily start taking people back, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed there for about two weeks, I would say, in-- in-- in this little town. They tried to take care of us as well as they could. Even for the lice-- we didn't get rid of the lice. They tried to get medicine for the typhus. They tried to feed us.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was very important. I mean, this is-- was the most important thing, as a matter of fact. If you to fight disease, you have to feed the people also, first of all. And they did, you know, try to find food. And we were lucky. At least I was lucky. I was so sick that I really couldn't eat, which was good because most-- many people overate. You know, your body is no longer used to eating rich foods. And some of them, you know, went into houses and saw meat lying there. And they ate it, took it and they-- all meat [INAUDIBLE] they ate it. [INAUDIBLE] got very sick. Some of them died. I couldn't really eat any food, so I ate some-- I remember eating mostly bread, dry bread, or biscuits. That was-- that's what I ate, and that's what also helped me to survive because my digestive system didn't-- wasn't put under that strain, you know. And after about 10 days to two weeks, the Americans decided to-- OK, these are mostly Dutch. There were some other people on the train with us, too. I don't know what happened to them. They stayed behind, probably. They were not Dutch. But we were mostly Dutch. And they were going to take us back to Holland because, you know, the-- there were other DPs in the area, too. DPs are displaced persons. They-- they didn't-- they had to put them up in camps, you know, and if they could repatriate people, the better, you know. And some of these people were-- really wanted to get back to Holland because they were Dutch, you know, and maybe they had family there or whatever.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were put on a-- unfortunately, they had no rolling stuff, nothing decent, so they put it on this open wagons. You know, those-- that they carry, say, a trailer-- it seemed like-- like open trailers we were put on, you know. And we used to travel only by day, and by night they put us up somewhere on the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they fed us, you know. But it didn't-- didn't help because it was sort of-- even if it was April, it seemed very hot to me. You know, we were in the open sun, you know, and-- and I got very sick on the way. And I remember that we never-- I never had enough water, and I needed water to drink, and there never was enough water because we were traveling. And we didn't have any bottles or anything like that, you know. So after about three days, we got to-- to Maastricht, to Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maastricht is in the very southern part of Holland, which-- why do they take us to Maastricht? Because [INAUDIBLE] Maastricht had been liberated by the Americans in September, 1944, September 4, 1944. Most of the rest of Holland hadn't, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And most of the-- the major part of Holland, as a matter of fact, had only been liberated when the war was over. It was isolated from the rest of-- from the rest of Europe. It was like a-- like a fortress. [? You ?] called it Fortress Holland, and-- and the Germans held it until the very end. But Maastricht was very lucky. You know, they were liberated then, and at that-- everything-- the Americans had everything there, and a lot of hospitals. I was very sick by the time we got there. And the rest of the people-- most-- many of the people were taken off the train. But those who could were taken to nearby-- like a dispersement camp, where, you know, where they checked out who you were, where you wanted to go to. And you couldn't go everywhere yet because this was in May-- May '45. And-- the major-- like Amsterdam-- these places had only been liberated, really, May 8, you know, when the war was over. So-- and-- and they also had suffered-- also had suffered from starvation. So you-- they couldn't send people there in any case. They had to wait. But we were taken off the train, my sister and myself, because we were very sick. I think I had-- had developed typhus by that time. And I was taken to one hospital, and my sister was taken to a different hospital. I was taken to a hospital which-- Maastricht was very Catholic. And at the time, all the nurses were nuns. And not only that, I was taken to a hospital which was inside a convent. It was a temporary hospital which had been established. And strangely enough, I was the only Jew-- the only kid there and the only Jew-- because the rest were all people who had-- men who had come back from under-- you know, who had served in the-- fought in the underground and been-- been caught and been brought back to Holland. And very soon, most of the people also were Catholics. Every morning there was sort of a-- a priest came to make mass, and they noticed that I didn't participate so they knew I was Jewish. You know, they didn't-- didn't bother me and I-- let me sleep. And they took all my clothes and burned it. You know, this was-- this was the thing to do. Of course I was left without anything, but that was OK, you know. I had just-- had pajamas or whatever. And basically, they tried to treat me, you know, and make sure that I didn't overeat, you know, because the food at that time was there. There was plenty of food. And they were very careful with the diet they gave me. And they tried to make it nice for me. And-- and-- what they did-- it was one big room where we lived, you know, in the hospital. There was, I don't know, maybe 20, 30 men in that room. And there was a balcony next to the room. And they put my bed on the balcony in order to be out in the fresh air. And they had a-- they ran sort of a-- a school-- no, not a nursery school-- a school they ran at the same time, the nuns, for Dutch kids in the area. So they used to come by in the morning and sing songs for me when I was lying in bed and-- in order to be sort of friendly and nice to sort of cheer me up. And after about two or three weeks or maybe-- yeah, something like that-- I was able to get up. I wasn't able to walk, [? actually, ?] and-- when I-- but I was able to get up. And I walked. Not very well, but I walked. And I wanted to go and see my sister. And she was in the regular hospital, also run by nuns, but in the regular hospital. And I went to see my sister. And the worst-- first thing she said to me, your teeth have grown. [LAUGHS] Because, you know, nothing grows in-- in-- in those conditions, you know. And I had-- the teeth-- my teeth had grown bigger, whatever, you know. That's the first thing she noticed.
